text: Hi, I'm Tobi from AWS. I'm joined today by Yevgeny from AlertLogic. Welcome, Yevgeny. Why don't you tell me a bit about AlertLogic?
AlertLogic is an SES security company providing 24-7 SOC for more than 4,000 customers of ours.
While doing this, we process about 25 petabytes of their data and on top of that we provide search functionality for both our customers and security engineers of our company.
Okay, and you're here today to tell me a bit about the system you use for search.
Yes, absolutely right. So, the system consists of two parts. It's data ingestion, which is trivial, and the data comes in from our agents and appliances from the customer fleet,
creates our kinesis streams, done a little bit of pre-processing, and ends up in our S3 data lake, which as I said, consists of 25 petabytes of data.
Okay, so this is your process data that's coming from your... Yes.
Yeah, great. On top of that we have a search engine, which is a rich SQL syntax with lots of data enrichment, which is parsing, meaning we extract meaning out of the data.
We search over, and the search engine is basically just a MapReduce engine consisting of several Lambda functions.
Okay, so let's walk through that. So the search request comes into your ECS cluster, is it?
So it looks very simple, yes. The search request comes into the ECS instance, and it does only validation and just planning of the execution.
Then it shadows it in a single Lambda function, which does job shadowing for the execution of the engine based on the data volume we have for this particular customer.
After that, it actually spins up thousands and thousands of filter functions, which do process data, filter data, and enrich it with the data which was requested.
All this data ends up in a different S3 bucket, which is then sent to Kinesis and to a different Lambda function to aggregate over.
All this is controlled by a single Lambda step function, which just keeps the state running.
Yeah, so this is managing the process and the result of that search. And the search results then are stored in S3 and passed back to your user via ECS.
Okay, great. And I can see you mentioned thousands of Lambda functions here. I imagine that search in that sense is well suited to Lambda in that it's a very spiky demand curve, isn't it?
It is. It is absolutely right. It is a spiky workload, meaning for a single search we have to spike from zero to thousand and sometimes even ten thousand Lambda functions.
Because what is search is searching over weeks or months or years of data, which is usually hundreds of terabytes.
So we have to scale it in a flash scale pretty much immediately and then tear it down.
Okay. So you've recently run a performance test on that search engine and you've got some results you want to share?
Yes, let me share some results.
So during the game day, which we ran the performance over, during an hour we ran 40,000 search queries.
While doing this we scaled from zero to 55,000 concurrent Lambda functions running all of those,
overall reaching 18 million executions during just an hour. And during all this exercise the SLO was flat and just in seconds like we promised,
which was five to 20 seconds for the execution.
Wow. So your Lambda fleet scaled from zero to 50,000 concurrent in an hour?
In an hour. And then when we stopped it went back to zero, saving us money.
Right. So I can see why you don't run your own server fleet for that in Lambda as well suited.
Absolutely. Running clusters, any type of clusters for us would be just ghost prohibitive in this case.
Okay. And the functions you execute in Lambda, which language to use?
So in AlertLogic we made a strategic choice to use Erlang all over the place.
Erlang is a runtime which is very easy to understand and very easy to use.
But on top of that, when we've integrated and provided Erlang as part of Lambda execution,
we found that it's actually very performant in terms of cold boot less than a second,
which is a very good fit for our use case.
Right. So you're taking advantage of the new support for Erlang in Lambda?
Yes.
Great. Okay. Well, thanks very much for sharing your solution today, Evgeny.
Thank you.
Thanks for watching. This is my architecture.
